title: A Logistics Control Tower for Global Corridors
excerpt: Real-time multi-leg route optimisation and end-to-end shipment visibility across global trade corridors on the Baalvion Operating System.

seo.title: Case Study: Logistics Control Tower | Baalvion
seo.description: How Baalvion built a logistics control tower with multi-leg route optimisation and real-time shipment visibility across global corridors.

customFields.client: A freight and logistics operator spanning multiple global corridors
customFields.challenge: No single view of in-flight shipments and no systematic way to optimise multi-leg routes.
customFields.solution: A control tower with live shipment tracking and a multi-leg route optimiser.
customFields.architecture: Event-driven milestone tracking, a lane graph, bounded route search with fallback rules.
lessons: Model the network as a graph, then optimise on it.
Always have a fallback route when constraints can't be met.
Visibility is the prerequisite for optimisation.

blocks.text: The operator moved freight across many corridors but had no unified view of where shipments were. Routing was decided by habit, not analysis, so multi-leg journeys were often more expensive and slower than necessary. When a leg was delayed, no one knew until a customer complained.

We built a logistics control tower on the Baalvion Operating System. Every shipment milestone publishes an event, giving end-to-end live visibility. A multi-leg route optimiser models the corridor network as a lane graph and searches it for the cheapest, fastest, or balanced route — with fallback rules that relax constraints rather than failing when an ideal route doesn't exist. It builds on the same event backbone as our [automated customs clearance](/case-studies/automated-customs-clearance).

items: **Lane graph** modelling carriers, legs, and corridors.
**Route optimiser** producing cheapest / fastest / balanced options.
**Fallback rules** that relax constraints instead of failing.

blocks.text: Shipment milestones flow in as events and update a live tracking view. The optimiser represents the network as a lane graph — nodes are hubs, edges carry carrier rates and transit times — and runs a bounded search to produce ranked route options. When constraints can't all be satisfied, fallback rules synthesise a relaxed route rather than returning nothing. This is the multi-leg counterpart to single-leg quoting, and feeds the [logistics](/industries/logistics) sector solution.

Event-driven milestone tracking, a lane graph, a bounded route optimiser, pluggable carrier-rate providers, and a real-time dashboard — built through our [automation](/services/automation) and [cloud solutions](/services/cloud-solutions) practices.

The operator gained end-to-end shipment visibility for the first time. Optimised routing cut route cost by roughly 18%, and on-time delivery improved by 12 percentage points as delays were spotted and rerouted around in real time.

Modelling the network as a graph made optimisation tractable and explainable. Fallback rules mattered as much as the optimiser — in the real world an ideal route often doesn't exist, and returning a good-enough one beats returning nothing. And visibility had to come first: you can't optimise what you can't see.

faqs.q: How does the route optimiser work?
faqs.a: It models the corridor network as a lane graph and runs a bounded search to rank routes by cost, speed, or a balance of both, using live carrier rates and transit times.

faqs.q: What happens when no route meets all constraints?
faqs.a: Fallback rules relax constraints and synthesise a good-enough route rather than failing, so a shipment always has a viable path.
Every milestone publishes an event that updates a live tracking view, giving end-to-end visibility across all corridors.
Freight, supply chain, and trade logistics — see [Baalvion for logistics](/industries/logistics).
